findings.summary: pipeline_reporter.py imports MultiProjectResult from infrastructure.core.multi_project, creating an upward reporting→core dependency that blurs layer boundaries
evidence: Line 17 of pipeline_reporter.py: `from infrastructure.core.multi_project import MultiProjectResult`
reporting is a consumer layer; importing a result dataclass from core.multi_project means the reporting module is coupled to orchestration internals rather than a stable data contract
findings.suggestion: Define a shared data transfer type (e.g. MultiProjectSummary) in a neutral location such as infrastructure.core.models or infrastructure.reporting itself, and have multi_project.py produce that type rather than having the reporter import from orchestration core.

findings.summary: health_check.py instantiates a module-level HealthCheckAPI singleton at import time, creating side effects on every import
evidence: Line 320: `_health_api = HealthCheckAPI()`  — executed unconditionally at module import
HealthCheckAPI.__init__ calls SystemHealthChecker.__init__ which records start_time and builds a check dict; this runs psutil.Process on import in any consumer
findings.suggestion: Use lazy initialization: make `_health_api` start as None and initialize it inside `get_health_api()` on first call. This eliminates import-time side effects.

findings.summary: performance.py also creates a module-level CodeProfiler singleton at import time, mirroring the same import-side-effect pattern as health_check.py
evidence: Line 524: `_global_monitor = CodeProfiler()` — executed unconditionally at import
This is a structural duplicate of the pattern in health_check.py; two sibling modules share the same architectural defect
findings.suggestion: Apply the same lazy-init fix: initialize inside `get_performance_monitor()` on first call rather than at module load.

findings.summary: Pipeline stage list uses bare tuple[0]/tuple[1]/tuple[2] positional indexing instead of a named dataclass or NamedTuple, making the protocol implicit and fragile
evidence: _build_stage_list returns list[tuple] and callers use `stage_spec[0]`, `stage_spec[1]`, `stage_spec[2] if len(stage_spec) > 2 else False` in three separate locations (_execute_pipeline, _resume_pipeline lines 187, 335, 364)
The repeated `len(stage_spec) > 2` guard is a convention smell indicating an ad-hoc protocol rather than a declared structure
findings.suggestion: Define a StageSpec dataclass or NamedTuple with fields (name, func, skip) and use it throughout _build_stage_list and its callers to make the protocol explicit.

findings.summary: infrastructure/reporting/__init__.py silently swallows ImportError for dashboard_generator, leaving three names out of __all__ with no warning, inconsistent with all sibling __init__ files
evidence: Lines 11-18: `try: from .dashboard_generator import ... except ImportError: pass` — bare pass with no log or fallback stub
generate_all_dashboards, generate_matplotlib_dashboard, generate_plotly_dashboard are listed in __all__ (lines 66-68) but may not exist at runtime if the import silently failed
findings.suggestion: Either guard the __all__ entries behind the same try/except, or log a warning on ImportError so callers can distinguish 'optional feature unavailable' from a real import bug.

findings.summary: _run_script catches bare Exception and returns False, discarding error type and subprocess details, while _execute_stage catches the same broad Exception for a different purpose — two different silent-swallow patterns in the same call chain
evidence: Lines 593-594: `except Exception as e: logger.error(SCRIPT_EXECUTION_FAILED...); return False` — exception is logged but not re-raised, callers receive only a bool

Lines 270-280: _execute_stage also catches bare Exception and converts to PipelineStageResult with error_message=str(e) — the two handlers compose silently, so a subprocess crash produces a logged error and a False bool but no propagation
findings.suggestion: These two layers serve different purposes (subprocess I/O vs stage orchestration) but both silently terminate the error chain. At minimum, distinguish OSError/TimeoutExpired from unexpected exceptions in _run_script so callers can tell 'script not found' from 'unexpected crash'.

findings.summary: integrity.py verification functions use inconsistent error strategies: verify_file_integrity returns Dict[str, bool] (encoded error), verify_output_integrity returns an IntegrityReport object, and verify_output_completeness returns Dict[str, Any] — three different return shapes for semantically similar operations
evidence: verify_file_integrity: returns Dict[str, bool] with False values encoding failure
verify_output_integrity: returns IntegrityReport with .overall_integrity bool and .issues list
verify_output_completeness: returns Dict[str, Any] with boolean fields and list fields mixed
findings.suggestion: Standardize the three functions onto IntegrityReport or a Protocol that provides a consistent .passed / .issues interface, so callers can handle all verification results uniformly.

findings.summary: extract_coverage_percentage in coverage_parser.py catches json.JSONDecodeError with debug-only logging inside a retry loop, silently treating malformed JSON the same as a missing file
evidence: Lines 342-347: `except json.JSONDecodeError as e: if is_infra: logger.debug(...)` — the decode error is only logged at DEBUG and only when is_infra=True; for project runs it is completely silent
A corrupt coverage.json will cause silent fallback to stdout regex parsing with no warning to the operator
findings.suggestion: Log JSONDecodeError at WARNING level unconditionally (not gated on is_infra), so operators see when a coverage file exists but is corrupt rather than silently getting a degraded result.

findings.summary: generate_test_report in pipeline_reporter.py is a zero-logic wrapper that ignores its input and returns a hardcoded placeholder path, documented with a comment admitting it delegates to a script
evidence: Lines 683-696: function accepts test_results and output_dir, creates the dir, then returns `output_dir / 'test_results.json'` — no data is written, no results are processed
Comment on line 693: `# This is a wrapper - actual test report generation is in scripts/01_run_tests.py`
findings.suggestion: Delete the function or replace it with the real implementation. A function that ignores its arguments and returns a path to a file it did not create is a contract violation that will silently mislead callers.

findings.summary: HealthCheckAPI is a one-concrete-implementation wrapper around SystemHealthChecker with no added policy: get_status delegates directly, get_status_summary restructures the dict, is_healthy is a one-liner
evidence: HealthCheckAPI.get_status (line 264): `return self.checker.get_health_status()` — pure delegation
HealthCheckAPI.is_healthy (line 282-285): `return bool(status['overall_status'] == 'healthy')` — could be a free function over SystemHealthChecker
No second implementation of the protocol exists or is hinted at in the codebase
findings.suggestion: Collapse HealthCheckAPI into SystemHealthChecker directly, or promote the free functions (quick_health_check, get_health_status, get_health_metrics) to use SystemHealthChecker without the intermediate class.

findings.summary: requests imported lazily inside health check network probe, but requests is a hard runtime dep used only for an optional liveness ping to httpbin.org
evidence: Line 172: `response = requests.get('https://httpbin.org/status/200', timeout=5)` inside `_check_network` — makes a real outbound HTTP call at health-check time

requests is listed as a root `[project]` dependency (pyproject.toml line 42) even though the only infrastructure consumers are this health probe plus publishing/LLM modules that could carry it themselves
findings.suggestion: Move the network liveness probe to an optional/best-effort helper that is not wired into the default health-check path; or gate it behind an explicit `network_check=True` flag so the hard dep on requests is justified by more than one optional call.

findings.summary: scipy is declared only in projects/code_project/pyproject.toml yet the root workspace has no workspace members, so it is never hoisted; infrastructure code that checks dependencies (environment.py) does not include it, creating a silent gap
evidence: projects/code_project/pyproject.toml declares `scipy>=1.10.0` but `[tool.uv.workspace] members = []` in root pyproject.toml means the sub-project is not a real workspace member
infrastructure/core/environment.py default `required_packages` list does not include scipy, so a missing scipy is never caught at environment-check time
findings.suggestion: Either add the sub-project as a proper uv workspace member, or move scipy into a `[dependency-groups]` entry in the root so it is managed alongside other optional deps.

findings.summary: python-dotenv and qrcode appear in both the optional `dotenv`/`steganography` dependency groups and in the `dev` group, creating redundant pinned entries
evidence: pyproject.toml lines 66-67: `dotenv` group declares `python-dotenv>=1.0.0`
pyproject.toml line 92: `dev` group independently declares `python-dotenv>=1.0.0`
pyproject.toml lines 70-72: `steganography` group declares `qrcode[pil]>=8.2`; `dev` group line 93 independently declares `qrcode[pil]>=8.2`
findings.suggestion: Remove the duplicate entries from the `dev` group and instead list the optional group names under `default-groups` or use `uv sync --group steganography --group dotenv` in CI, so there is a single source of truth for each dependency version constraint.

findings.summary: infrastructure/reporting/coverage_parser.py and infrastructure/reporting/coverage_reporter.py are called from the main test runner script but have zero test files targeting them
evidence: Grep for 'coverage_parser|coverage_reporter' in tests/ returns no files
scripts/01_run_tests.py line 39 imports from `infrastructure.reporting.coverage_parser`, making it a high-import critical path module
infrastructure/reporting/ has 14 source files; only 10 corresponding test files exist in tests/infra_tests/reporting/
findings.suggestion: Add test_coverage_parser.py and test_coverage_reporter.py that exercise `extract_failed_tests`, `check_cov_datafile_support`, and `parse_coverage_json` with synthetic pytest stdout strings and a temp JSON file.

findings.summary: Multiple test files use time.sleep() to create measurable durations, coupling test correctness to wall-clock timing
evidence: test_progress.py has 12+ time.sleep() calls ranging from 0.01s to 0.25s
test_logging_utils.py line 229: `time.sleep(0.15)` to ensure duration > `min_duration_to_log` — couples test to the 0.1s threshold constant
test_security.py line 332: `time.sleep(1.1)` — pushes beyond a rate-limit window, making the test brittle on slow CI
findings.suggestion: Accept an injectable clock/time source in the production functions under test, or restructure the assertions to check relative ordering and result shape rather than elapsed wall time, eliminating the sleep dependency.

findings.summary: tests/integration/ exists but is not included in the default pytest testpaths, so cross-module verification is silently skipped in normal runs
evidence: pyproject.toml line 116: `testpaths = ["tests"]` — this should include integration/, but the directory was found via grep only in test_full_pipeline.py timing search
The `test-infra` script entry in pyproject.toml explicitly passes `tests/infra_tests/` not `tests/`; `test-project` points to projects/; neither covers `tests/integration/`
findings.suggestion: Either add `tests/integration` to `testpaths` (or include it in a named `integration` run in CI), or mark `test_full_pipeline.py` with `@pytest.mark.integration` and document how to run it, to make coverage of cross-module paths explicit.

findings.summary: Several simple getter/forwarding functions carry multi-line docstrings with Args/Returns/Example sections that add no information beyond the function signature
evidence: logging_utils.py `get_logger` (lines 215-228): 13-line docstring for a 10-line function that calls `setup_logger`; the docstring restates what the type signature already says
logging_utils.py `log_success` (lines 370-379): 10-line docstring including an Example block for a 3-line function that formats a string and calls `logger.info`

environment.py `check_python_version` (lines 50-55): docstring says 'Verify Python 3.8+ is available. Returns: True if Python version is 3.8 or higher' — exact restatement of the function name and return type
findings.suggestion: Trim docstrings on trivial functions to a single summary line. Reserve Args/Returns/Example sections for functions where parameter semantics or return shape are non-obvious.

findings.summary: Utility functions in environment.py and logging_utils.py emit logger.info() at the start of every function body, producing noisy logs that restate function names
evidence: environment.py line 56: `logger.info('Checking Python version...')` — restates the function name
environment.py line 79: `logger.info('Checking dependencies...')` — restates the function name
environment.py line 195: `logger.info('Checking build tools...')` — restates the function name
environment.py line 262: `logger.info('Setting up directory structure...')` — restates the function name
environment.py line 424: `logger.info('Setting environment variables...')` — restates the function name
findings.suggestion: Replace mandatory entry-log lines with `logger.debug()` calls or remove them entirely; callers in the pipeline already emit stage-level headers. Reserve `logger.info` for meaningful state changes (e.g., what was actually installed or what was missing).

findings.summary: health_check.py instantiates a global HealthCheckAPI at module import time, performing side-effectful resource probes on every import
evidence: Lines 319-320: `_health_api = HealthCheckAPI()` at module scope — `HealthCheckAPI.__init__` creates a `SystemHealthChecker` that stores `time.time()` immediately

Three module-level wrapper functions (`quick_health_check`, `get_health_status`, `get_health_metrics`, lines 328-344) simply forward to `_health_api`, adding no logic — classic pass-through wrapper pattern

The `HealthCheckAPI` class itself wraps `SystemHealthChecker` with only forwarding methods (`get_status`, `get_status_summary`, `is_healthy`, `get_metrics`) — two layers of single-implementation wrappers with no added policy
findings.suggestion: Remove the global `_health_api` instance and the redundant `HealthCheckAPI` wrapper; expose `SystemHealthChecker` directly or use a module-level lazy factory. Eliminate the three pass-through module functions.

findings.summary: infrastructure/validation/ contains 22 files spanning at least three distinct concern groups — PDF/markdown validation, documentation auditing (doc_scanner, doc_quality, doc_accuracy, doc_completeness, repo_scanner, audit_orchestrator), and output structure checking — with no sub-package separation
evidence: 22 files in a flat directory (Glob result shows no sub-directories)

doc_* group (7 files: doc_models, doc_scanner, doc_discovery, doc_accuracy, doc_completeness, doc_quality, doc_models) has high intra-group coupling but lives alongside unrelated pdf_validator and output_validator
issue_categorizer.py and audit_orchestrator.py are orchestration/categorization concerns distinct from both document scanning and PDF/markdown validation, yet share the flat namespace
findings.suggestion: Create sub-packages infrastructure/validation/doc_audit/ (doc_models, doc_scanner, doc_discovery, doc_accuracy, doc_completeness, doc_quality, audit_orchestrator, issue_categorizer, repo_scanner) and infrastructure/validation/output/ (output_validator, pdf_validator, markdown_validator, figure_validator), leaving cli.py and integrity.py at the validation/ level.

findings.summary: infrastructure/reporting/ has four modules (coverage_parser.py, coverage_reporter.py, test_summary_generator.py, summary_generator.py, manuscript_overview.py) that are NOT re-exported from __init__.py, creating an inconsistent public surface
evidence: infrastructure/reporting/__init__.py exports from pipeline_reporter, error_aggregator, executive_reporter, output_reporter, output_organizer, multi_project_reporter, dashboard_generator — but not from coverage_parser, coverage_reporter, test_summary_generator, summary_generator, or manuscript_overview
scripts/01_run_tests.py imports `from infrastructure.reporting.coverage_parser import ...` — bypassing the package's declared public surface
tests/infra_tests/reporting/test_manuscript_overview.py and test_summary_generator.py import their modules directly, consistent with the __init__ omission
findings.suggestion: Either add the missing modules to __init__.py __all__ to complete the public surface, or explicitly document (e.g. in the module docstring) that these modules are internal and intended for direct import only. Inconsistency between the two patterns increases navigation cost.

findings.summary: infrastructure/core/ contains observability subsystems (health_check, performance, performance_monitor) that blur the core boundary
evidence: health_check.py exposes HealthCheckAPI with get_status/get_status_summary/get_metrics — a full REST-like monitoring layer inside core/
performance.py contains CodeProfiler (cProfile/tracemalloc), StagePerformanceTracker, and benchmark_function — three distinct observability concerns in one file under core/
A separate performance_monitor.py also exists alongside performance.py, indicating the concern was already split once without resolving the boundary
findings.suggestion: Move health_check.py and performance*.py into an infrastructure/monitoring/ or infrastructure/observability/ sub-package to keep core/ focused on logging, config, errors, environment, and pipeline primitives

findings.summary: pipeline_reporter.py imports MultiProjectResult from infrastructure.core.multi_project, creating a downward reporting→core dependency that blurs layer ownership
evidence: Line 16: `from infrastructure.core.multi_project import MultiProjectResult` — reporting layer reaches into core for a domain result type
generate_multi_project_summary_report() takes MultiProjectResult as its first argument, coupling the reporting module to core's execution model
findings.suggestion: Define MultiProjectResult (and PipelineStageResult) in a shared infrastructure/models.py or infrastructure/core/results.py that both core/multi_project.py and reporting/pipeline_reporter.py import from, so the reporting layer does not depend on core's orchestration module

findings.summary: HealthCheckAPI wraps SystemHealthChecker with three nearly identical pass-through methods and lives inside core/ with no apparent external REST consumer
evidence: HealthCheckAPI.get_status() calls self.checker.get_health_status() with no transformation
HealthCheckAPI.is_healthy() calls self.get_status() then checks one field — a one-liner with no policy
Module-level singleton _health_api = HealthCheckAPI() is instantiated at import time, carrying network I/O risk into the import graph
findings.suggestion: Remove HealthCheckAPI and expose get_health_status/quick_health_check/get_health_metrics as module-level functions directly on SystemHealthChecker (or as free functions), eliminating the wrapper class that adds no policy

findings.summary: The exit-code-2 skip-handling block is copy-pasted verbatim between _execute_pipeline and _resume_pipeline
evidence: _execute_pipeline lines 196–203: `if result.exit_code == 2: result = PipelineStageResult(... success=True, exit_code=2)`

_resume_pipeline lines 371–380: identical `if hasattr(result, 'exit_code') and result.exit_code == 2: result = PipelineStageResult(... success=True, exit_code=2)` — even adds a redundant hasattr guard since result is always PipelineStageResult
findings.suggestion: Extract a _normalize_skip_result(result: PipelineStageResult) -> PipelineStageResult helper that both execution paths call; remove the redundant hasattr guard since the type is statically known

findings.summary: generate_multi_project_summary_report computes slowest/fastest project with deeply nested conditional generator expressions that obscure the seam
evidence: Lines 119–144: `'slowest_project': (max((n, summary['projects'][n]['duration']) for n in summary['projects'] if ...), key=lambda x: x[1])[0] if any(... for n in summary['projects']) else None` — the guard condition re-iterates the same dict a second time
The same any(...) guard pattern is repeated for both slowest and fastest, adding 8 lines of repeated logic
findings.suggestion: Extract project durations into a filtered list once, then use simple min/max calls; this collapses both guarded expressions to two readable lines and eliminates the double iteration

findings.summary: _resume_pipeline filters remaining stages into a list then re-checks skip_condition inside the execution loop over that same list
evidence: Lines 332–346: loop builds `remaining` list by skipping stages where skip_condition is True
Lines 361–366: execution loop over `remaining` again checks `if skip_condition: continue` — dead guard since skipped stages were never added to remaining
findings.suggestion: Remove the redundant skip_condition check inside the execution loop over remaining; the filtering loop above already guarantees remaining contains only non-skipped stages

findings.summary: extract_failed_tests implements five sequential fallback parsing strategies as inline code blocks under repeated if-not-failed_tests guards, making the function a 135-line monolith
evidence: Five consecutive blocks each guarded by `if not failed_tests:` (lines 110, 129, 151, 179), each repeating the pattern of scanning combined_output and appending to failed_tests
Method 4 (timeout) contains a nested double-loop that re-scans the entire output for context lines even though the outer for-loop already landed on the timeout line
findings.suggestion: Define each fallback as a named private function _parse_failures_from_section, _parse_failures_verbose, etc., then drive them from a short loop: `for strategy in _FAILURE_STRATEGIES: result = strategy(combined_output); if result: return result`

findings.summary: _validate_single_file repeats the same try/except + QualityIssue construction + append pattern four times, varying only the called function and issue_type string
evidence: Lines 179–193 (code validation), 196–210 (directory), 213–227 (imports), 230–244 (placeholders): each block is structurally identical — call validator function, iterate results, append QualityIssue with hardcoded issue_type, except: logger.warning
The only variation is the validator function, the issue_type string literal, and the severity value
findings.suggestion: Replace the four blocks with a data-driven loop over a list of (flag, validator_fn, issue_type, severity) tuples, calling a shared _run_quality_validator(content, md_file, repo_root, results, ...) helper

findings.summary: performance.py conflates two distinct design abstractions — resource-usage sampling (PerformanceMonitor/StagePerformanceTracker) and function-level profiling (CodeProfiler/benchmark_function) — in a single 600-line file
evidence: PerformanceMonitor tracks start/stop CPU/memory for pipeline stages; CodeProfiler wraps cProfile.Profile() and tracemalloc for function-level profiling — fundamentally different lifecycles and callers
Module-level _global_monitor = CodeProfiler() creates a singleton for function profiling, while StagePerformanceTracker is instantiated per-pipeline — mixed ownership patterns in the same file
benchmark_function() is defined twice: once as CodeProfiler.benchmark_function (line 437) and once as a module-level free function (line 581) that creates a throw-away CodeProfiler instance
findings.suggestion: Split into performance_tracker.py (PerformanceMonitor, StagePerformanceTracker, performance_context, get_system_resources) and code_profiler.py (CodeProfiler, monitor_performance decorator, benchmark_function, profile_memory_usage); remove the duplicate module-level benchmark_function

findings.summary: validate_build_artifacts, verify_output_completeness, and check_file_permissions all use Dict[str, Any] as primary output, then immediately create typed local list aliases as a workaround for the untyped container
evidence: validate_build_artifacts lines 471–473: `expected_list: List[str] = validation['expected_files']; missing_list: List[str] = validation['missing_files']` — aliases created purely to satisfy type checker on a dict that should be a dataclass
verify_output_completeness lines 571–572: same pattern with missing_outputs/incomplete_outputs
check_file_permissions line 525: `issues_list: List[str] = permissions['issues']` — same pattern, third occurrence
findings.suggestion: Replace the Dict[str, Any] return types with dataclasses (BuildArtifactValidation, OutputCompleteness, PermissionCheck); the typed alias workaround pattern disappears entirely and the API becomes self-documenting

findings.summary: health_check.py instantiates HealthCheckAPI at module level, creating a global singleton whose check chain includes a live network request to httpbin.org
evidence: Line 320: `_health_api = HealthCheckAPI()` executed at import time
_check_network() calls `requests.get('https://httpbin.org/status/200', timeout=5)` — live network I/O on any import of this module
quick_health_check() and get_health_status() route through the global singleton, making every caller share state and side effects
findings.suggestion: Remove the module-level singleton; make get_health_api() create a new HealthCheckAPI on demand (or use functools.lru_cache with explicit reset), and lazy-init _check_network only when explicitly requested rather than as part of every get_health_status() call
